messages.content:
I have a spread sheet that I enter data in rows from column A to K
I would like to have a VBA code that can do the following.
When I double click on any cell in column A
A popUp will ask "Do you want to duplicate the Task"
If I answer Yes then
It wil select all the cells in the next row below from column column A to K that contain values in column A
then past the selection another row below then
clear the contents of the row above.
For example if I click on a cell in column A in row 12,
it will select from row 13 downwards from A to K all the cells that have values then
copy and past the selection in row 14 and then
clear the contents of row 13 so that I have an empty row just ubder row 12 where I had started the process by double clicking.
If it can also copy the value from row 12 column A to row 13 column A that would be great too.

You can use the following VBA code to achieve the desired functionality:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Clear the row above the double-clicked cell
            Rows(Target.Row - 1).ClearContents
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the original values
            Target.Offset(1).ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

Here's how to add the code to your workbook:

1. Press `ALT + F11` to open the VBA editor.
2. In the Project Explorer window, find and double-click the worksheet where you want to enable this functionality.
3. Copy and paste the code into the code window on the right.
4. Close the VBA editor.

Now, whenever you double-click on any cell in column A of the chosen worksheet, the code will prompt you to duplicate the task. If you choose "Yes", it will duplicate the task as described in your question.

How will I write this correctly:    
            Target LastRow.Offset(1).ClearContents

To write the code correctly, you need to separate the `Target` and `LastRow` variables. Also, you can't directly apply the `Offset` method to the `Target` variable without specifying which direction and number of rows to offset.

Here's the correct way to clear the contents of the cell below the `LastRow`:

```vba
Target.Offset(1).ClearContents
```

Make sure to replace `Target` with the actual cell reference you want to clear the contents of, and adjust the `Offset` parameters if needed.
I want to clear the row below the LastRow

To clear the row below the `LastRow`, you can use the `Offset` property with `RowOffset` set to 1. Here's the updated code:

```vba
LastRow.Offset(1).ClearContents
```

Replace `LastRow` with the actual variable or cell reference representing the last row in your worksheet.

This code will clear the contents of all cells in the row below `LastRow`, effectively creating an empty row just below your data.

I believe this is what I want. Can you describe the process: Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the values just below the Last Row
            LastRow.Offset(1).ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub

This VBA code is designed to run when a double-click event occurs in the worksheet. Here is a description of the process:

1. The code is triggered by a double-click event on any cell in the worksheet.
2. The `Target` parameter represents the cell that was double-clicked.
3. The code checks if the double-click occurred in column A (`Target.Column = 1`).
4. If the double-click is in column A, a message box asks the user if they want to duplicate the task.
5. If the user selects "Yes" (`response = vbYes`), the process continues.
6. The code finds the last used row in column A (`LastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row`).
7. The values in cells from the row below the `Target` cell to the last used row in column K are shifted down by one row (`Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)`).
8. The contents of the row just below the `LastRow` are cleared (`LastRow.Offset(1).ClearContents`).
9. The value from the row above the `Target` cell is copied to the `Target` cell (`Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value`).

Overall, this code allows you to duplicate a task by double-clicking on a cell in column A, and it performs the necessary actions of shifting down the data, clearing the row below the last used row, and copying the value above to the current row.
error: Invalid qualifier, on the line lastRow.Offset(1).ClearContents

Apologies for the confusion. The error is occurring because `lastRow` is a variable representing the row number, not an object that can be used with the `Offset` property.

To clear the row below the last used row, you can use the `Rows` object directly like this:

```vba
Rows(lastRow + 1).ClearContents
```

This will clear the contents of all cells in the row following the last used row.

Here's the updated code:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the row below the Last Row
            Rows(lastRow + 1).ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

This updated code should resolve the issue and clear the row below the last used row correctly.
The code did everything it should except it did not clear the contents of the row just below the Last Row

Apologies for the oversight. To clear the contents of the row just below the last used row, you can use the `Offset` method along with the `Resize` method:

```vba
Rows(lastRow + 1).Resize(1).ClearContents
```

Here's the updated code with the correction:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the row below the Last Row
            Rows(lastRow + 1).Resize(1).ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

Now, when you double-click on any cell in column A, the code will duplicate the task by shifting the values down, clearing the row below the last used row, and copying the value from the row above to the current row.
The code is clearing the contents of the very last row and not the row just below the row where I double clicked

Apologies for the confusion. To clear the row just below the clicked row, you can use the `Offset` property with a `RowOffset` of 1. Here's the corrected code:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the row below the clicked row
            Target.Offset(1).EntireRow.ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

With this updated code, the row below the double-clicked row will be cleared, and the rest of the functionality remains the same. Now when you double-click on any cell in column A, it will duplicate the task, shift the values, clear the row below the clicked row, and copy the value from the row above to the current row.
how als can I stop the double click from going into edit mode in a cell

To prevent the double-click event from entering edit mode in a cell, you can use the `Cancel` parameter of the `Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick` event. By setting `Cancel = True`, you can stop the default behavior of entering edit mode.

Here's the modified code to include preventing the edit mode on double-click:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_BeforeDoubleClick(ByVal Target As Range, Cancel As Boolean)
    Dim response As VbMsgBoxResult
    Dim lastRow As Long
    
    ' Check if double click occurred in column A
    If Target.Column = 1 Then
        ' Prevent entering edit mode on double-click
        Cancel = True
        
        ' Ask user if they want to duplicate the task
        response = MsgBox("Do you want to duplicate the task?", vbQuestion + vbYesNo)
        
        If response = vbYes Then
            ' Get the last used row in column A
            lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Row
            
            ' Shift the values one row down starting from the double-clicked cell
            Range(Target.Offset(1), Cells(lastRow, 11)).Copy Destination:=Target.Offset(2)
            
            ' Clear the row below the clicked row
            Target.Offset(1).EntireRow.ClearContents
            
            ' Copy value from row above to current row
            Target.Value = Target.Offset(-1).Value
        End If
    End If
End Sub
```

With this modification, upon a double-click in column A, the code will duplicate the task, shift the values, clear the row below the clicked row, copy the value from the row above to the current row, and prevent entering the default edit mode.

Now, when you double-click on any cell in column A, it will perform the desired actions without entering edit mode.

please correct this for me: Sub GoBackToLastActiveSheet()
    If LastActiveSheet <> "" Then
        Sheets(LastActiveSheet).Activate
Else
Sheets(Day).Activate
    End If
End Sub

Certainly! Here's the corrected version of the code:

```vba
Sub GoBackToLastActiveSheet()
    Dim LastActiveSheet As String
    Dim Day As String
    
    LastActiveSheet = "Sheet1" ' Replace with the name of your last active sheet
    Day = "Sheet2" ' Replace with the name of the sheet you want to activate if LastActiveSheet is not available
    
    If LastActiveSheet <> "" And SheetExists(LastActiveSheet) Then
        Sheets(LastActiveSheet).Activate
    Else
        Sheets(Day).Activate
    End If
End Sub

Function SheetExists(sheetName As String) As Boolean
    On Error Resume Next
    SheetExists = Not Sheets(sheetName) Is Nothing
    On Error GoTo 0
End Function
```

In this code, we first declare the variables `LastActiveSheet` and `Day`. `LastActiveSheet` should contain the name of the last active sheet you want to go back to. `Day` should contain the name of the sheet you want to activate if `LastActiveSheet` is not available.

The function `SheetExists` is included to check if a sheet with the given name exists. It returns `True` if the sheet exists, and `False` otherwise.

In the `GoBackToLastActiveSheet` subroutine, we check if `LastActiveSheet` is not an empty string and also if a sheet with that name exists using the `SheetExists` function. If it does, we activate that sheet using the `Sheets(LastActiveSheet).Activate` method. Otherwise, we activate the `Day` sheet using `Sheets(Day).Activate`.

Replace `"Sheet1"` and `"Sheet2"` with the actual names of your sheets. Make sure the names are enclosed within double quotes.

Now, when you run the `GoBackToLastActiveSheet` macro, it will activate the last active sheet if available and activate the `Day` sheet otherwise.
How would you write this in the simplest form of VBA - Activate worksheet 'Day'

To activate the worksheet named "Day" in the simplest form of VBA, you can use the following code:

```vba
Sheets("Day").Activate
```

This code will activate the worksheet named "Day" in the current workbook.

Please note that if you have multiple workbooks open, you need to make sure the workbook containing the "Day" sheet is active or use the workbook reference to activate the sheet.

If there is no go back vaue stored, I want the go back to activate the Planner Page. The code is not doing this: Option Explicit

Public LastActiveSheet As String

Sub GoBackToLastActiveSheet()
    If LastActiveSheet <> "" Then
        Sheets(LastActiveSheet).Activate
Else
Sheets("Day").Activate
    End If
End Sub

Apologies for the confusion. There seems to be an issue with the code where the `LastActiveSheet` variable is not being stored properly. 

To resolve this, you can modify the code to use the `Worksheet_Activate` event to update the `LastActiveSheet` variable whenever a sheet is activated. Here's the updated code:

```vba
Option Explicit

Public LastActiveSheet As String

Private Sub Workbook_SheetActivate(ByVal Sh As Object)
    LastActiveSheet = Sh.Name
End Sub

Sub GoBackToLastActiveSheet()
    If LastActiveSheet <> "" Then
        Sheets(LastActiveSheet).Activate
    Else
        Sheets("Day").Activate
    End If
End Sub
```

With this updated code, the `LastActiveSheet` variable will be updated whenever a sheet is activated in the workbook, ensuring that the correct sheet is activated when running the `GoBackToLastActiveSheet` macro.

Make sure to place the `Workbook_SheetActivate` event handler code in the code module for the workbook where you want to track the last active sheet. The `GoBackToLastActiveSheet` macro can be placed in any standard module in the same workbook.

Now, the `GoBackToLastActiveSheet` macro will activate the last active sheet if available, and if no last active sheet is stored, it will activate the "Day" sheet.

Note: The `Workbook_SheetActivate` event will only update the `LastActiveSheet` variable when a sheet is activated in the workbook while the workbook is open. If the workbook is closed and reopened, the `LastActiveSheet` variable will not retain its value.
